title: Popol Vuh: The Creation of Humanity
description: The Mayan Genesis: the gods' repeated attempts to create beings who can worship them, culminating in humans made from corn.
introduction: The Popol Vuh is the creation epic of the K'iche' Maya — the most complete surviving account of Mesoamerican cosmology. It tells of the gods' repeated failures to create beings capable of worship: first mud people (who dissolved), then wooden people (who were destroyed in a flood), and finally corn people — humanity as we know it. The narrative interweaves creation with the heroic saga of the Hero Twins in the underworld of Xibalba.

translation.original_text: Are u xe oher tzih waral K'iche' ub'i'. Waral xchiqatz'ib'aj wi, xchiqatikib'a' wi oher tzih.
translation.text: This is the beginning of the ancient word, here in the place called K'iche'. Here we shall write, we shall begin the ancient stories.

translation.original_text: Nab'e chi k'o wi ri kaj, k'o nay puch u wachulew. Xa u tukel k'o wi ri kaj. Xa katzininak, xa kasilanik.
translation.text: First there was only sky, and there was also the surface of the earth. Nothing stirred. Nothing moved. It was silent and still.

translation.original_text: Ta xkib'an winaq, ixim ub'aqil, ixim u kik'el.
translation.text: Then they made people. Corn was their flesh, corn was their blood.
